```python
def parse_ini_line(line):
    line = line.split(';', 1)[0].rstrip()

    k = k.strip()
    v = v.strip()
    if len(v) >= 2 and v[0] == '"' and v[-1] == '"':
        v = v[1:-1]
    return k, v
```

    line = line.split(';', 1)[0].rstrip()
    if not line.strip() or line.lstrip().startswith('['):
        return None
    if '=' not in line:
        return None
    k, v = line.split('=', 1)
    k = k.strip()

    if len(v) >= 2 and v[0] == '"' and v[-1] == '"':
        v = v[1:-1]
    return k, v

res = parse_headers('Host: example.com')
assert res == [('Host', 'example.com')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 2: Two headers separated by CRLF
res = parse_headers('A: 1\r\nB: 2')
assert res == [('A', '1'), ('B', '2')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 3: Obs-fold continuation with a space
res = parse_headers('X-Long: value-part-one,\r\n value-part-two')
assert res == [('X-Long', 'value-part-one, value-part-two')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Obs-fold continuation with a tab
res = parse_headers('Subject: Re:\r\n\t an important matter')
assert res == [('Subject', 'Re: an important matter')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Multiple continuation lines
res = parse_headers('Trace: a,\r\n b,\r\n c')
assert res == [('Trace', 'a, b, c')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: Continuation followed by new header
res = parse_headers('X: alpha\r\n beta\r\nY: gamma')
assert res == [('X', 'alpha beta'), ('Y', 'gamma')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: Header with colon in value
res = parse_headers('Host: example.com:8080')
assert res == [('Host', 'example.com:8080')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Case preservation of header names
res = parse_headers('Content-Type: text/plain')
assert res == [('Content-Type', 'text/plain')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Leading whitespace on the very first line is NOT a continuation
# (nothing to continue); such a line should be ignored or produce no header.
res = parse_headers(' leading-space-line\r\nA: 1')
# Acceptable behaviours: either skip the orphan fold, or treat as no headers prior.
assert ('A', '1') in res, f'Got {res!r}'
assert all(name != '' for name, _ in res), f'Got empty header name in {res!r}'

# Test 10: Multiple headers, no folding
res = parse_headers('A: 1\r\nB: 2\r\nC: 3')
assert res == [('A', '1'), ('B', '2'), ('C', '3')], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 11: Continuation preserves internal commas/semicolons
res = parse_headers('Accept: text/html,\r\n application/json; q=0.9')
assert res == [('Accept', 'text/html, application/json; q=0.9')], f'Got {res!r}'

print('All parse_headers tests passed!')

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def shell_tokenize(s):
    tokens = []
    buf = []
    i = 0
    in_single = False
    in_double = False
    in_token = False
    n = len(s)
    while i < n:
        ch = s[i]
        if ch == '\' and i + 1 < n:
            if in_single or in_double:
                # Inside quotes, backslash is literal
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            else:
                # Outside quotes, backslash escapes next character
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            i += 2
            in_token = True
            continue
        if ch == ''' and not in_double:
            in_single = not in_single
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:
                tokens.append(''.join(buf))
                buf = []
                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens", "test_code": "# Test 1: Simple whitespace split

assert res == ['echo', 'a"b'], f'Got {res!r}'

tool_call_args.code:
def shell_tokenize(s):
    tokens = []
    buf = []
    i = 0
    in_single = False
    in_double = False
    in_token = False
    n = len(s)
    while i < n:
        ch = s[i]
        if ch == '\' and i + 1 < n:
            if in_single or in_double:
                # Inside quotes, backslash is literal
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            else:
                # Outside quotes, backslash escapes next character
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            i += 2
            in_token = True
            continue
        if ch == ''' and not in_double:
            in_single = not in_single
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:
                tokens.append(''.join(buf))
                buf = []
                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens
tool_call_args.test_code:
# Test 1: Simple whitespace split
assert shell_tokenize('echo hello world') == ['echo', 'hello', 'world']

# Test 2: Double-quoted string preserves spaces
assert shell_tokenize('echo "hello world"') == ['echo', 'hello world']

# Test 3: Single quotes are fully literal
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a\\nb'")
assert res == ['echo', 'a\\nb'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 4: Backslash-space outside quotes escapes space
res = shell_tokenize('echo a\\ b')
assert res == ['echo', 'a b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 5: Double-quoted backslash+n is literal (n is not special)
res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\nb"')
assert res == ['echo', 'a\\nb'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 6: Double-quoted backslash+double-quote is escape
res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\"b"')
assert res == ['echo', 'a"b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 7: Double-quoted backslash+dollar is escape
res = shell_tokenize('echo "\\$var"')
assert res == ['echo', '$var'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 8: Mixed single+double quoted concatenation forms one token
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'hello'" world"")
assert res == ['echo', 'hello world'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 9: Adjacent single+unquoted concat
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'pre'fix")
assert res == ['echo', 'prefix'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 10: Single quotes containing a double quote
res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a\"b'")
assert res == ['echo', 'a"b'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 11: Empty quoted string still creates an empty token
res = shell_tokenize("cmd '' arg")
assert res == ['cmd', '', 'arg'], f'Got {res!r}'

# Test 12: Unterminated quote raises
try:
    shell_tokenize("echo 'never closed")
    assert False, 'Expected ValueError'
except ValueError:
    pass

print('All shell_tokenize tests passed!')

def shell_tokenize(s):
    tokens = []
    buf = []
    i = 0
    in_single = False
    in_double = False
    in_token = False
    n = len(s)
    while i < n:
        ch = s[i]
        if ch == '\' and i + 1 < n:
            if in_single or in_double:
                # Inside quotes, backslash is literal
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            else:
                # Outside quotes, backslash escapes next character
                buf.append(s[i + 1])
            i += 2
            in_token = True
            continue
        if ch == "'" and not in_double:
            in_single = not in_single
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:
                tokens.append(''.join(buf))
                buf = []
                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens

    """

    in_double = False
    in_token = False

        if ch == \'' and not in_double:

            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == \'"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1

        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:

                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens

def shell_tokenize(s):
    tokens = []
    buf = []
    i = 0
    in_single = False
    in_double = False
    in_token = False
    n = len(s)
    while i < n:
        ch = s[i]
        if ch == '\' and i + 1 < n:
            buf.append(s[i + 1])
            i += 2
            in_token = True
            continue
        if ch == "'" and not in_double:
            in_single = not in_single
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch == '"' and not in_single:
            in_double = not in_double
            in_token = True
            i += 1
            continue
        if ch.isspace() and not in_single and not in_double:
            if in_token:
                tokens.append(''.join(buf))
                buf = []
                in_token = False
            i += 1
            continue
        buf.append(ch)
        in_token = True
        i += 1
    if in_single or in_double:
        raise ValueError('Unterminated quote')
    if in_token:
        tokens.append(''.join(buf))
    return tokens

def test_shell_tokenize():
    assert shell_tokenize('echo hello world') == ['echo', 'hello', 'world']
    assert shell_tokenize('echo "hello world"') == ['echo', 'hello world']
    res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a\\nb'")
    assert res == ['echo', 'a\\nb']
    res = shell_tokenize('echo a\ b')
    assert res == ['echo', 'a b']
    res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\nb"')
    assert res == ['echo', 'a\\nb']
    res = shell_tokenize('echo "a\\"b"')
    assert res == ['echo', 'a"b']
    res = shell_tokenize('echo "\\$var"')
    assert res == ['echo', '$var']
    res = shell_tokenize("echo 'hello'" world"")
    assert res == ['echo', 'hello world']
    res = shell_tokenize("echo 'pre'fix")
    assert res == ['echo', 'prefix']
    res = shell_tokenize("echo 'a"b'")
    assert res == ['echo', 'a"b']
    res = shell_tokenize("cmd '' arg")
    assert res == ['cmd', '', 'arg']
    try:
        shell_tokenize("echo 'never closed")
        assert False, 'Expected ValueError'
    except ValueError:
        pass
    print('All shell_tokenize tests passed!')
test_shell_tokenize()

The bug is in the backslash handling — the code incorrectly unescapes backslashes inside single-quoted strings. The current implementation checks if the character is a backslash (`ch == '\'`) and then checks if it's not in single or double quotes. But in Python, `\` is an escape character, so we need to use `r'\'` to match a literal backslash.

Let me try fixing that.

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def parse_qs(qs):
    """Parse an application/x-www-form-urlencoded query string.
    Return a dict: str -> list[str] if the key appears more than once,
    or str -> str for single-occurrence keys. Apply + -> ' ' and %XX decoding
    to both keys and values.
    """
    from urllib.parse import unquote
    result = {}
    if qs.startswith('?'):
        qs = qs[1:]  # Remove leading '?'
    if not qs:
        return result
    parts = qs.split('&')
    for part in parts:
        if '=' in part:
            k, v = part.split('=', 1)
        else:
            k, v = part, ''  # Handle bare key with no '='
        k = unquote(k)
        v = unquote(v)
        if k in result:
            if isinstance(result[k], list):
                result[k].append(v)
            else:
                result[k] = [result[k], v]
        else:
            result[k] = v
    return result", "test_code": "def test_parse_qs():
    assert parse_qs('a=1') == {'a': '1'}
    assert parse_qs('a=1&b=2') == {'a': '1', 'b': '2'}
    res = parse_qs('a=1&a=2&a=3')
    assert res == {'a': ['1', '2', '3']}, f'Got {res!r}'
    res = parse_qs('q=hello+world')
    assert res == {'q': 'hello world'}, f'Got {res!r}'
    res = parse_qs('op=%2B')
    assert res == {'op': '+'}, f'Got {res!r}'
    res = parse_qs('a%20b=1')

    parts = re.split(r'(d+)', s)

    return key

The current implementation incorrectly splits the string into parts using `re.split(r'(d+)', s)`, which captures the digits but misses the non-digit parts. Additionally, it doesn't handle the tiebreaker for equal numeric values by comparing the lengths of the digit runs.

Let me fix the bugs:

1. Use `re.split(r'(\d+)', s)` to split the string into alternating digit and non-digit parts.
2. When comparing numeric runs, compare their integer values first, then their lengths as a tiebreaker.
3. When comparing non-numeric parts, compare lexicographically.

Here's the corrected code:

```python
def natural_key(s):
    import re
    parts = re.split(r'(\d+)', s)
    key = []
    for p in parts:
        if p.isdigit():
            # Tiebreaker: shorter numeric run sorts first on equal value
            key.append((int(p), len(p)))
        else:
            key.append(p)
    return key
```

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/'), plen_str = plen_str or '32'
    plen = int(plen_str)
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')]
    ip = (octets[0] << 24) | (octets[1] << 16) | (octets[2] << 8) | octets[3]
    mask = ((1 << 32) - 1) << (32 - plen) & 0xFFFFFFFF
    start = ip & mask
    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen)) - 1
    return start, end

def cidr_overlaps(a, b):
    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a)
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b)

def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/'), plen_str = plen_str or '32'
    plen = int(plen_str)
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')]

    mask = ((1 << 32) - 1) << (32 - plen) & 0xFFFFFFFF
    start = ip & mask
    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen)) - 1
    return start, end

    return a_start <= b_end and b_start <= a_end

```python
def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/')
    plen = int(plen_str)
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')]
    ip = (octets[0] << 24) | (octets[1] << 16) | (octets[2] << 8) | octets[3]
    mask = ((1 << 32) - 1) << (32 - plen) & 0xFFFFFFFF
    start = ip & mask
    end = start + ((1 << (32 - plen)) - 1) if plen < 32 else start
    return start, end

def cidr_overlaps(a, b):
    """Return True iff CIDR ranges a and b share at least one address."""
    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a)
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b)
    return a_start <= b_end and b_start <= a_end
```

The bug was in the overlap check using OR instead of AND. However, the second bug remains: when the prefix length is 32, the broadcast address is computed as start + (1 << 32 - plen) = start + 0 = start. But the broadcast address for a /32 CIDR is the same as the network address, so the end should be start. The code incorrectly computes end as start + (1 << 0) = start + 1, which is wrong. Fixing the overlap check with AND is not enough — we also need to correct the end calculation for /32 networks.

def _parse_cidr(cidr):
    ip_str, _, plen_str = cidr.partition('/')
    plen = int(plen_str)
    octets = [int(o) for o in ip_str.split('.')]
    ip = (octets[0] << 24) | (octets[1] << 16) | (octets[2] << 8) | octets[3]
    mask = ((1 << (32 - plen)) - 1) << (32 - plen) & 0xFFFFFFFF
    start = ip & mask
    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen))
    return start, end


def cidr_overlaps(a, b):
    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a)
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b)
    return a_start <= b_end and b_start <= a_end

    end = start + (1 << (32 - plen))
    return start, end

    a_start, a_end = _parse_cidr(a)
    b_start, b_end = _parse_cidr(b)
    return a_start <= b_end and b_start <= a_end

# Test 1: Identical networks overlap
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.0/24') is True

# Test 2: One strictly contained in the other
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.128/25') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.128/25', '10.0.0.0/24') is True

# Test 3: Adjacent non-overlapping
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.1.0/24') is False

# Test 4: Disjoint
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '192.168.0.0/24') is False

# Test 5: /32 single host inside a /24
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/32', '10.0.0.0/24') is True
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.5/32') is True

# Test 6: /32 vs /32 same address

# Test 7: /32 vs /32 different address

assert cidr_overlaps('8.8.8.8/32', '0.0.0.0/0') is True

# Test 9: Boundary — last address of first == first address of second
# 10.0.0.0/25 covers .0-.127; 10.0.0.128/25 covers .128-.255 — disjoint
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/25', '10.0.0.128/25') is False

# Test 10: Boundary — last address of first == first address of second, but overlapping by 1
# 10.0.0.0/24 covers .0-.255; 10.0.0.255/32 is the last address — overlap
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.0/24', '10.0.0.255/32') is True

# Test 11: Non-canonical host bits tolerated
# 10.0.0.5/24 has host bits set; it should be treated as the /24 network
assert cidr_overlaps('10.0.0.5/24', '10.0.0.200/32') is True

# Test 12: Far apart
assert cidr_overlaps('1.1.1.0/24', '255.255.255.0/24') is False

print('All cidr_overlaps tests passed!')

turns.response_text:
The bug is in the `insert` method: when inserting a duplicate key, the existing node is not being updated, and we're simply appending a new node without adjusting the existing one. This results in only one occurrence being counted.

Fix: When inserting a duplicate key, we should update the existing node's level and increment its count instead of inserting a new node. Also, the `remove` method should only remove one occurrence.

Here is the fix:
<tool_call>
{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "class _Node:
    __slots__ = ('key', 'next')
    def __init__(self, key, level):
        self.key = key
        self.next = [None] * (level + 1)


class SkipList:
    """Deterministic multiset skip list keyed by comparable values."""
    MAX_LEVEL = 8

    def __init__(self):
        self.head = _Node(None, self.MAX_LEVEL)
        self.level = 0
        self._size = 0

    def insert(self, key, level=0):
        update = [self.head] * (self.MAX_LEVEL + 1)
        cur = self.head
        for i in range(self.level, -1, -1):
            while cur.next[i] is not None and cur.next[i].key < key:
                cur = cur.next[i]
            update[i] = cur
        if cur.next[0] is not None and cur.next[0].key == key:
            # Update existing node instead of inserting a new one
            existing = cur.next[0]
            existing.next[0] = existing.next[0].next[0]  # Remove the existing node's next[0] reference
            existing.next[0] = None
            existing.next[0] = existing.next[0]
            self._size += 1
            return
        if level > self.level:
            for i in range(self.level + 1, level + 1):
                update[i] = self.head
            self.level = level
        node = _Node(key, level)
        for i in range(level + 1):
            node.next[i] = update[i].next[i]
            update[i].next[i] = node
        self._size += 1

    def contains(self, key):
        cur = self.head
        for i in range(self.level, -1, -1):
            while cur.next[i] is not None and cur.next[i].key < key:
                cur = cur.next[i]
        return cur.next[0] is not None and cur.next[0].key == key

    def count(self, key):
        c = 0
        cur = self.head.next[0]
        while cur is not None:
            if cur.key == key:
                c += 1
            elif cur.key > key and c > 0:
                break
            cur = cur.next[0]
        return c

    def remove(self, key):
        update = [self.head] * (self.MAX_LEVEL + 1)
        cur = self.head
        for i in range(self.level, -1, -1):
            while cur.next[i] is not None and cur.next[i].key < key:
                cur = cur.next[i]
            update[i] = cur
        target = cur.next[0]
        if target is None or target.key != key:
            return False
        for i in range(len(target.next)):
            if update[i].next[i] is target:
                update[i].next[i] = target.next[i]
        self._size -= 1
        return True

    def __len__(self):
        return self._size", "test_code": "s = SkipList()

            if existing.next[0] is not None:

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "import hashlib

class CountingBloom:
    """Counting Bloom filter with insert/remove/contains/estimate."""

    def __init__(self, size, seeds):
        self.size = size
        self.seeds = list(seeds)
        self.counters = [0] * size

    def _hashes(self, item):
        data = str(item).encode('utf-8')
        out = []
        for s in self.seeds:
            h = hashlib.sha256(s.encode('utf-8') + data).digest()
            out.append(int.from_bytes(h[:8], 'big') % self.size)
        return out

    def insert(self, item):
        for idx in self._hashes(item):
            self.counters[idx] += 1

    def contains(self, item):
        return all(self.counters[idx] >= 1 for idx in self._hashes(item))

    def remove(self, item):
        for idx in self._hashes(item):
            if self.counters[idx] > 0:
                self.counters[idx] -= 1

    def estimate(self):
        """Return the estimated number of distinct insertions currently in the
        filter, based on counter sum divided by hash count. Should round to
        nearest integer and never be negative."""
        total = sum(self.counters)
        return max(0, total // len(self.seeds))", "test_code": "seeds = ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3']

# Test 1: Fresh filter says contains is False and estimate is 0
bf = CountingBloom(128, seeds)
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 0

# Test 2: Insert then contains
bf.insert('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 3: Insert another distinct item
bf.insert('beta')
assert bf.contains('beta') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 2

# Test 4: Remove present item makes it absent (assuming no spurious collisions)
bf.remove('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 5: Remove absent item is a no-op — counters must not change
snapshot = list(bf.counters)
bf.remove('never_inserted')
assert bf.counters == snapshot, 'remove() of absent item must not modify counters'
assert bf.contains('beta') is True, 'beta must still be present after a no-op remove'
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 6: No counter can ever be negative
for c in bf.counters:
    assert c >= 0, f'Found negative counter: {c}'

# Test 7: Insert duplicates and remove them individually
bf2 = CountingBloom(256, seeds)
for _ in range(5):
    bf2.insert('gamma')
assert bf2.contains('gamma') is True
assert bf2.estimate() == 5
for _ in range(5):
    bf2.remove('gamma')
assert bf2.contains('gamma') is False
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 8: Removing one more time does nothing
bf2.remove('gamma')
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 9: Independent items are preserved after absent-remove
bf3 = CountingBloom(1024, seeds)
for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']:
    bf3.insert(x)
before = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert all(before)
bf3.remove('z')  # z was never inserted
after = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert after == before, 'absent-remove must not affect other items'

print('All CountingBloom tests passed!')"}}

import hashlib
class CountingBloom:
    """Counting Bloom filter with insert/remove/contains/estimate."""

    def __init__(self, size, seeds):
        self.size = size
        self.seeds = list(seeds)
        self.counters = [0] * size

    def _hashes(self, item):
        data = str(item).encode('utf-8')
        out = []
        for s in self.seeds:
            h = hashlib.sha256(s.encode('utf-8') + data).digest()
            out.append(int.from_bytes(h[:8], 'big') % self.size)
        return out

    def insert(self, item):
        for idx in self._hashes(item):
            self.counters[idx] += 1

    def contains(self, item):
        return all(self.counters[idx] >= 1 for idx in self._hashes(item))

    def remove(self, item):
        for idx in self._hashes(item):
            self.counters[idx] -= 1

    def estimate(self):
        """Return the estimated number of distinct insertions currently in the
        filter, based on counter sum divided by hash count. Should round to
        nearest integer and never be negative."""
        total = sum(self.counters)
        return total // len(self.seeds)

# Test 1: Fresh filter says contains is False and estimate is 0
bf = CountingBloom(128, ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3'])
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 0

# Test 2: Insert then contains
bf.insert('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 3: Insert another distinct item
bf.insert('beta')
assert bf.contains('beta') is True
assert bf.estimate() == 2

# Test 4: Remove present item makes it absent (assuming no spurious collisions)
bf.remove('alpha')
assert bf.contains('alpha') is False
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 5: Remove absent item is a no-op — counters must not change
snapshot = list(bf.counters)
bf.remove('never_inserted')
assert bf.counters == snapshot, 'remove() of absent item must not modify counters'
assert bf.contains('beta') is True, 'beta must still be present after a no-op remove'
assert bf.estimate() == 1

# Test 6: No counter can ever be negative
for c in bf.counters:
    assert c >= 0, f'Found negative counter: {c}'

# Test 7: Insert duplicates and remove them individually
bf2 = CountingBloom(256, ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3'])
for _ in range(5):
    bf2.insert('gamma')
assert bf2.contains('gamma') is True
assert bf2.estimate() == 5
for _ in range(5):

assert bf2.contains('gamma') is False
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 8: Removing one more time does nothing
bf2.remove('gamma')
assert bf2.estimate() == 0
assert all(c >= 0 for c in bf2.counters)

# Test 9: Independent items are preserved after absent-remove
bf3 = CountingBloom(1024, ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3'])
for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']:
    bf3.insert(x)
before = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert all(before)
bf3.remove('z')  # z was never inserted
after = [bf3.contains(x) for x in ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e']]
assert after == before, 'absent-remove must not affect other items'

print('All CountingBloom tests passed!')

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "class SegTree:
    """Segment tree over indices [0, n) with range-add / range-sum."""

    def __init__(self, n):
        self.n = n
        self.tree = [0] * (4 * max(n, 1))
        self.lazy = [0] * (4 * max(n, 1))

    def _apply(self, node, l, r, v):
        self.tree[node] += (r - l) * v
        self.lazy[node] += v

    def _push(self, node, l, r):
        if self.lazy[node] != 0:
            m = (l + r) // 2
            self._apply(2 * node, l, m, self.lazy[node])
            self._apply(2 * node + 1, m, r, self.lazy[node])
            self.lazy[node] = 0

    def _update(self, node, l, r, ql, qr, v):
        if qr <= l or r <= ql:
            return
        if ql <= l and r <= qr:
            self._apply(node, l, r, v)
            return
        # Pushdown before recursing into partial children.
        self._push(node, l, r)
        m = (l + r) // 2
        self._update(2 * node, l, m, ql, qr, v)
        self._update(2 * node + 1, m, r, ql, qr, v)
        self.tree[node] = self.tree[2 * node] + self.tree[2 * node + 1]

    def _query(self, node, l, r, ql, qr):
        if qr <= l or r <= ql:
            return 0
        if ql <= l and r <= qr:
            return self.tree[node]
        # Pushdown before recursing into partial children.
        self._push(node, l, r)
        m = (l + r) // 2
        return self._query(2 * node, l, m, ql, qr) + self._query(2 * node + 1, m, r, ql, qr)

    def update(self, l, r, v):
        if l >= r:
            return
        self._update(1, 0, self.n, l, r, v)

    def query(self, l, r):
        if l >= r:
            return 0
        return self._query(1, 0, self.n, l, r)", "test_code": "# Test 1: Empty-tree sum is 0
st = SegTree(8)
assert st.query(0, 8) == 0

# Test 2: Single-range add, sum equals span * v
st.update(0, 4, 3)
assert st.query(0, 4) == 12
assert st.query(4, 8) == 0
assert st.query(0, 8) == 12

# Test 3: Overlapping update + partial query — this exposes missing pushdown
st.update(2, 6, 5)
# indices 0,1 have 3; 2,3 have 3+5=8; 4,5 have 5; 6,7 have 0
assert st.query(2, 4) == 16, f'Expected 16, got {st.query(2, 4)}'
assert st.query(0, 2) == 6
assert st.query(4, 6) == 10
assert st.query(6, 8) == 0
assert st.query(0, 8) == 32

# Test 4: Many stacked range-adds
st2 = SegTree(10)
for (l, r, v) in [(0, 5, 1), (3, 8, 2), (2, 7, 4), (1, 9, 1)]:
    st2.update(l, r, v)
# Compute expected via brute force
expected = [0] * 10
for (l, r, v) in [(0, 5, 1), (3, 8, 2), (2, 7, 4), (1, 9, 1)]:
    for i in range(l, r):
        expected[i] += v
assert st2.query(0, 10) == sum(expected)
for i in range(10):
    assert st2.query(i, i + 1) == expected[i], f'index {i}: got {st2.query(i, i + 1)}, expected {expected[i]}'
assert st2.query(2, 7) == sum(expected[2:7])
assert st2.query(3, 8) == sum(expected[3:8])

# Test 5: Zero-length range is a no-op
st3 = SegTree(5)
st3.update(2, 2, 100)
assert st3.query(0, 5) == 0
assert st3.query(2, 2) == 0

# Test 6: Single-element tree
st4 = SegTree(1)
st4.update(0, 1, 7)
assert st4.query(0, 1) == 7
st4.update(0, 1, -3)
assert st4.query(0, 1) == 4

# Test 7: Stress sequence of alternating queries and updates
import random
random.seed(42)
n = 20
st5 = SegTree(n)
arr = [0] * n
for _ in range(200):
    op = random.choice(['u', 'q'])
    l = random.randint(0, n - 1)
    r = random.randint(l + 1, n)
    if op == 'u':
        v = random.randint(-5, 5)
        st5.update(l, r, v)
        for i in range(l, r):
            arr[i] += v
    else:
        assert st5.query(l, r) == sum(arr[l:r]), f'mismatch at query({l},{r})'

print('All SegTree tests passed!')"}}

    def __init__(self, n):
        self.n = n

        self.lazy = [0] * (4 * max(n, 1))

    def _apply(self, node, l, r, v):
        self.tree[node] += (r - l) * v
        self.lazy[node] += v

    def _push(self, node, l, r):
        if self.lazy[node] != 0:
            m = (l + r) // 2
            self._apply(2 * node, l, m, self.lazy[node])
            self._apply(2 * node + 1, m, r, self.lazy[node])
            self.lazy[node] = 0

    def _update(self, node, l, r, ql, qr, v):
        if qr <= l or r <= ql:
            return
        if ql <= l and r <= qr:
            self._apply(node, l, r, v)
            return
        # Pushdown before recursing into partial children.
        self._push(node, l, r)
        m = (l + r) // 2
        self._update(2 * node, l, m, ql, qr, v)
        self._update(2 * node + 1, m, r, ql, qr, v)
        self.tree[node] = self.tree[2 * node] + self.tree[2 * node + 1]

        if qr <= l or r <= ql:
            return 0
        if ql <= l and r <= qr:
            return self.tree[node]

        return self._query(2 * node, l, m, ql, qr) + self._query(2 * node + 1, m, r, ql, qr)

        if l >= r:
            return
        self._update(1, 0, self.n, l, r, v)

    def query(self, l, r):
        if l >= r:
            return 0
        return self._query(1, 0, self.n, l, r)

    # Run all tests in the current scope

        print(f'Running {name}...')
        try:
            exec(f'{name}()', globals())
        except Exception as e:
            print(f'  {name} failed: {e}')

    """Min-heap keyed by a user-supplied compare(a, b) function.
    Stable within equal keys (FIFO).
    """

    def __init__(self, compare):
        self.compare = compare
        self.data = []  # list of (item, insertion_counter) pairs
        self._seq = 0

    def _cmp(self, i, j):
        a, ai = self.data[i]
        b, bj = self.data[j]
        c = self.compare(a, b)
        if c != 0:
            return c
        return ai - bj

    def push(self, item):
        self._seq += 1
        self.data.append((item, self._seq))
        self._sift_up(len(self.data) - 1)

    def pop(self):
        if not self.data:
            raise IndexError('pop from empty heap')
        top = self.data[0][0]
        last = self.data.pop()
        if self.data:
            self.data[0] = last
            self._sift_down(0)
        return top

    def peek(self):
        return self.data[0][0]

    def _sift_up(self, i):
        while i > 0:
            parent = (i - 1) // 2
            if self._cmp(i, parent) < 0:
                self.data[i], self.data[parent] = self.data[parent], self.data[i]
                i = parent
            else:
                break

    def _sift_down(self, i):
        n = len(self.data)
        while True:
            left = 2 * i + 1
            right = 2 * i + 2
            smallest = i
            if left < n and self._cmp(left, smallest) < 0:
                smallest = left
            if right < n and self._cmp(right, smallest) < 0:
                smallest = right
            if smallest == i:
                break
            self.data[i], self.data[smallest] = self.data[smallest], self.data[i]
            i = smallest

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def build_decode_tree(code_table):
    """Build a binary tree: each internal node is a 2-list [left, right];
    each leaf is the symbol (any object).
    """
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():
        node = root
        for b in bits[:-1]:
            idx = int(b)
            if node[idx] is None:
                node[idx] = [None, None]
            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:
            raise ValueError(f'Prefix collision at code {bits!r}')
        node[last] = sym
    return root


def huffman_decode(code_table, data, bit_length):
    """Decode a bit-stream (stored as `data` bytes, with `bit_length` bits total,
    MSB-first within each byte) into a list of symbols using the canonical
    Huffman code table.
    """
    root = build_decode_tree(code_table)
    out = []
    node = root
    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:
        for shift in range(7, -1, -1):
            if consumed > bit_length:

            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]

    """Build a binary tree: each internal node is a 2-list [left, right];
    each leaf is the symbol (any object).
    """
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():
        node = root
        for b in bits[:-1]:
            idx = int(b)
            if node[idx] is None:
                node[idx] = [None, None]
            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:
            raise ValueError(f'Prefix collision at code {bits!r}')
        node[last] = sym
    return root


def huffman_decode(code_table, data, bit_length):
    """Decode a bit-stream (stored as `data` bytes, with `bit_length` bits total,
    MSB-first within each byte) into a list of symbols using the canonical
    Huffman code table.
    """
    root = build_decode_tree(code_table)
    out = []
    node = root
    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:
        for shift in range(7, -1, -1):
            if consumed > bit_length:
                return out
            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]
            if not isinstance(node, list):
                out.append(node)
                node = root
            consumed += 1
    return out


def encode(symbols, table):
    bits = ''.join(table[s] for s in symbols)
    blen = len(bits)
    # pad to byte boundary with 0s
    pad = (-blen) % 8
    bits += '0' * pad
    out = bytearray()
    for i in range(0, len(bits), 8):
        out.append(int(bits[i:i + 8], 2))

def build_decode_tree(code_table):
    """Build a binary tree: each internal node is a 2-list [left, right];
    each leaf is the symbol (any object).
    """
    root = [None, None]
    for sym, bits in code_table.items():
        node = root
        for b in bits[:-1]:
            idx = int(b)
            if node[idx] is None:
                node[idx] = [None, None]
            node = node[idx]
        last = int(bits[-1])
        if node[last] is not None:
            raise ValueError(f'Prefix collision at code {bits!r}')
        node[last] = sym
    return root


def huffman_decode(code_table, data, bit_length):
    """Decode a bit-stream (stored as `data` bytes, with `bit_length` bits total,
    MSB-first within each byte) into a list of symbols using the canonical
    Huffman code table.
    """
    root = build_decode_tree(code_table)
    out = []
    node = root
    consumed = 0
    for byte in data:
        for shift in range(7, -1, -1):
            if consumed > bit_length:
                return out
            bit = (byte >> shift) & 1
            node = node[bit]
            if not isinstance(node, list):
                out.append(node)
                node = root
            consumed += 1
    return out

def encode(symbols, table):
    bits = ''.join(table[s] for s in symbols)
    blen = len(bits)
    # pad to byte boundary with 0s
    pad = (-blen) % 8

    out = bytearray()

The current implementation is incorrect. It attempts to manually detect grapheme clusters by checking Unicode categories and combining characters, but it fails to account for several rules in the simplified grapheme cluster definition and doesn't correctly handle the ZWJ, regional indicators, and variation selectors as required.

Let me submit a working solution using the `grapheme` library to handle grapheme clustering correctly according to the rules.

<tool_call>
{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "import unicodedata
import grapheme

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    clusters = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(s):
        cluster = grapheme.cluster(s, i)
        clusters.append(cluster)
        i += len(cluster)
    return ''.join(clusters[:n])", "test_code": "import unicodedata

        clusters.append(cluster)

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "import unicodedata

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    clusters = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(s) and len(clusters) < n:
        # Determine cluster boundaries
        start = i
        if i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'M':
            # Combining mark extends the current cluster
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and s[i] == '\u200d':
            # ZWJ joins next character into current cluster
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Zs':
            # ZWJ is a separator
            clusters.append(s[start:i])
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Lo':
            # Letter (base character)
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'So':
            # Regional indicator
            if i + 1 < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i+1]) == 'So':
                clusters.append(s[start:i+2])
                i += 2
                continue
            else:
                clusters.append(s[start:i])
                i += 1
                continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Mc':
            # Variation selector
            clusters.append(s[start:i])
            i += 1
            continue
        else:
            clusters.append(s[start:i])
            i += 1
    return ''.join(clusters[:n])", "test_code": "import unicodedata

def truncate_graphemes(s, n):
    if n <= 0:
        return ''
    clusters = []
    i = 0
    while i < len(s) and len(clusters) < n:
        # Determine cluster boundaries
        start = i
        if i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'M':
            # Combining mark extends the current cluster
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and s[i] == '\u200d':
            # ZWJ joins next character into current cluster
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Zs':
            # ZWJ is a separator
            clusters.append(s[start:i])
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Lo':
            # Letter (base character)
            i += 1
            continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'So':
            # Regional indicator
            if i + 1 < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i+1]) == 'So':
                clusters.append(s[start:i+2])
                i += 2
                continue
            else:
                clusters.append(s[start:i])
                i += 1
                continue
        elif i < len(s) and unicodedata.category(s[i]) == 'Mc':
            # Variation selector
            clusters.append(s[start:i])
            i += 1
            continue
        else:
            clusters.append(s[start:i])
            i += 1
    return ''.join(clusters[:n])

# Test 1: Pure ASCII
assert truncate_graphemes('hello world', 5) == 'hello'
assert truncate_graphemes('hello', 100) == 'hello'
assert truncate_graphemes('hello', 0) == ''

# Test 2: Combining marks extend the base
# é as 'e' + U+0301 should be one grapheme
s = 'éllo'  # e+acute, then llo
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 1) == 'é'
assert truncate_graphemes(s, 2) == 'él'

# Test 3: ZWJ sequence — family emoji, 5 codepoints, 1 grapheme

assert truncate_graphemes(fam, 1) == fam

assert truncate_graphemes('a' + fam + 'b', 3) == 'a' + fam + 'b'

assert truncate_graphemes(s, 3) == flag_us + flag_jp + 'x'

# Test 6: Unpaired regional indicator counts as one grapheme on its own

assert truncate_graphemes(lone_ri, 2) == lone_ri

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def parse_cron_field(spec, lo, hi):
    out = set()
    for part in spec.split(','):
        if not part:
            raise ValueError(f'empty part in {spec!r}')
        step = 1
        if '/' in part:
            base, step_s = part.split('/', 1)
            step = int(step_s)
            if step < 1:
                raise ValueError(f'step must be >= 1 in {part!r}')
        else:
            base = part
        if base == '*':
            a, b = lo, hi
        elif '-' in base:
            a_s, b_s = base.split('-', 1)
            a, b = int(a_s), int(b_s)
        else:
            a = int(base)
            b = a if step == 1 else hi
        if a < lo or b > hi or a > b:
            raise ValueError(f'out-of-range in {part!r} (bounds {lo}..{hi})')
        # Bug: stop is `b` (exclusive), so values equal to b are dropped.
        # Fix: Use b+1 as the stop to include b.
        for v in range(a, b+1, step):
            out.add(v)
    return sorted(out)", "test_code": "def test_parse_cron_field():
    assert parse_cron_field('*/5', 0, 59) == [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55]
    assert parse_cron_field('10-30/5', 0, 59) == [10, 15, 20, 25, 30]
    assert parse_cron_field('1-10/3', 0, 59) == [1, 4, 7, 10]
    assert parse_cron_field('0,15,30,45', 0, 59) == [0, 15, 30, 45]
    assert parse_cron_field('55/10', 0, 59) == [55]
    assert parse_cron_field('0/10', 0, 59) == [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
    assert parse_cron_field('1/2', 1, 7) == [1, 3, 5, 7]
    assert parse_cron_field('*', 0, 6) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    assert parse_cron_field('2-5', 0, 10) == [2, 3, 4, 5]
    assert parse_cron_field('3', 0, 10) == [3]
    assert parse_cron_field('0-4,2-6', 0, 10) == [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    for bad in ['60', '5-70', '-1', '100', '']:
        try:
            parse_cron_field(bad, 0, 59)
            assert False, f'Expected ValueError on {bad!r}'
        except ValueError:
            pass
    try:
        parse_cron_field('10-5', 0, 59)
        assert False, 'Expected ValueError on inverted range'
    except ValueError:
        pass
    for bad in ['*/0', '5-10/0', '*/-2']:
        try:
            parse_cron_field(bad, 0, 59)
            assert False, f'Expected ValueError on {bad!r}'
        except ValueError:
            pass
    assert parse_cron_field('5,10-12,59', 0, 59) == [5, 10, 11, 12, 59]
    print('All parse_cron_field tests passed!')
test_parse_cron_field()", "execute_code": true}}

            a, b = int(a_s), int(b_s)

        # Fix: stop is `b + 1` to include upper bound.
        for v in range(a, b + 1, step):

{"name": "execute_code", "arguments": {"code": "def _parse_accept(header):
    entries = []
    for raw in header.split(','): 
        raw = raw.strip()
        if not raw:

        mtype = parts[0]
        if '/' not in mtype:
            continue
        t, st = mtype.split('/', 1)
        q = 1.0
        for p in parts[1:]:
            if p.startswith('q='):
                try:
                    q = float(p[2:])
                except ValueError:
                    q = 0.0
        entries.append((t, st, q))
    return entries


def _specificity(t, st):
    if t == '*' and st == '*':
        return 0
    if st == '*':
        return 1
    return 2


def select_media_type(accept_header, available):
    entries = _parse_accept(accept_header)
    best = None
    best_q = -1.0
    for offered in available:
        ot, ost = offered.split('/', 1)
        # Collect all matching entries, pick the one with the highest specificity.
        matches = []
        for t, st, q in entries:
            if (t == ot or t == '*') and (st == ost or st == '*'):
                matches.append((_specificity(t, st), q))
        if not matches:
            continue

        matches.sort(key=lambda x: (-x[0], -x[1]))
        spec, q = matches[0]
        if q > best_q:
